_description: Curated TNBC molecular-subtype classifications from Lehmann et al., JCI 2011 (PMID: 21633166) and the 2016 reclassification (Lehmann et al., PLOS ONE 2016, PMID: 27310713). The 2011 paper defined six subtypes from gene-expression deconvolution: BL1 (basal-like 1, DNA damage response), BL2 (basal-like 2, growth-factor signaling), IM (immunomodulatory), M (mesenchymal), MSL (mesenchymal stem-like), and LAR (luminal androgen receptor). The 2016 update collapsed this to four (TNBCtype-4): BL1, BL2, M, LAR — IM and MSL were re-attributed to infiltrating immune / stromal contamination rather than being distinct tumour-cell subtypes. We carry both labels per cell line so users can choose which framework to apply. Lehmann's published cell-line panel covered ~25 TNBC lines; this anchor list is restricted to lines with explicit assignments in the original tables. Cell-line names use stripped-uppercase form for lookup; the processing script maps to DepMap ACH IDs via cellLineMetadata.strippedCellLineName.
source_2011: Lehmann et al. JCI 2011 PMID 21633166, Table 4 + Supplementary Table S5 (TNBCtype-6 classifications)
source_2016: Lehmann et al. PLOS ONE 2016 PMID 27310713 (TNBCtype-4 reclassification)

subtypes_2011.BL1: Basal-like 1: cell cycle, DNA damage response, ATR/BRCA pathway. Often platinum/PARP-sensitive.
subtypes_2011.BL2: Basal-like 2: growth-factor signaling (EGF, NGF, MET, IGF1R, Wnt/β-catenin), glycolysis/gluconeogenesis. Often growth-factor-receptor-targetable.
subtypes_2011.IM: Immunomodulatory: immune-cell signaling (Th1/Th2, NK, B-cell, antigen processing). Largely an infiltrate-derived signal — re-attributed in 2016.
subtypes_2011.M: Mesenchymal: cell-motility, EMT, ECM-receptor interaction, growth-factor receptors.
subtypes_2011.MSL: Mesenchymal stem-like: shares M signature plus low proliferation, growth-factor signaling, claudin-low. Re-attributed to stromal infiltration in 2016.
subtypes_2011.LAR: Luminal androgen receptor: AR-driven, often PIK3CA mutant. Bicalutamide / enzalutamide candidate.
